questions.question: Explain the key differences between UDP and TCP protocols in terms of reliability, ordering, speed, and overhead. Also, mention specific use cases where each protocol is preferred.
keyPoints: UDP is connectionless, unreliable, no ordering, faster, lower overhead; TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, provides ordering
UDP suitable for streaming, VoIP, DNS, video calls where speed matters over reliability
TCP suitable for web browsing, email, file transfer where reliability is critical
TCP provides flow control via sliding window, UDP has no flow control mechanism

questions.question: Describe the TCP three-way handshake process and explain why it is necessary for establishing a reliable connection.
keyPoints: Step 1: Client sends SYN segment with sequence number
Step 2: Server responds with SYN-ACK (acknowledges SYN and sends its sequence number)
Step 3: Client sends ACK to confirm, connection established
Three-way handshake ensures both parties are ready, synchronizes sequence numbers, and prevents stale connections

questions.question: Explain the TCP sliding window mechanism for flow control. Include details about how the receiver advertises its window size, how the sender uses this information, and what happens when the window becomes zero. Also, discuss the concept of the Receiver Window (rwnd) and how TCP prevents the sender from overwhelming the receiver.
keyPoints: Sliding window allows sender to transmit multiple packets before receiving acknowledgments, improving throughput
Receiver advertises available buffer space (rwnd) in TCP header, sender respects this limit
Zero window scenario: sender stops sending and uses probe packets (window probes) to detect when receiver buffer frees up
Window scaling option in TCP for handling high-bandwidth networks with large RTTs

questions.question: Discuss the TCP congestion control mechanisms in detail. Explain the difference between slow start, congestion avoidance, and fast retransmit phases. Also, describe how TCP Tahoe, Reno, and NewReno handle packet loss differently, and discuss the significance of the congestion window (cwnd) and threshold (ssthresh).
keyPoints: Slow start: cwnd starts at 1 MSS, exponentially increases until ssthresh; Congestion avoidance: cwnd increases linearly (1 packet per RTT)
Fast retransmit: on 3 duplicate ACKs, retransmit lost segment without waiting for timeout; fast recovery follows
TCP Tahoe: sets ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = 1 MSS on timeout; TCP Reno: fast recovery, cwnd = ssthresh after fast retransmit
Congestion window (cwnd) limits data sender can transmit; threshold (ssthresh) determines transition between slow start and congestion avoidance
